items.question: Who uses hierarchical clustering in practice?
items.answer: Data scientists, bioinformaticians, market researchers, and ML engineers use it for exploratory segmentation, heatmap ordering, embedding visualization, and taxonomy building when nested structure matters more than training speed.

items.question: What does a dendrogram represent?
items.answer: A dendrogram is a tree whose leaves are individual observations and whose internal nodes record merge order; branch height shows the dissimilarity at which two sub-trees were combined.

items.question: When is hierarchical clustering preferable to k-means?
items.answer: When k is unknown, you want a multi-scale view of groupings from one run, or you need interpretable nested structure for visualization—especially on moderate-sized datasets (hundreds to a few thousand points).

items.question: Where does linkage enter the algorithm?
items.answer: At each agglomerative step, linkage defines the distance between every pair of current clusters from their constituent points, determining which two clusters merge next.

items.question: Why does single linkage create chaining?
items.answer: Single linkage uses the minimum cross-cluster point distance, so one close bridge between two dense regions can merge them early, producing elongated, chain-like clusters.

items.question: How do you obtain k clusters from a fitted hierarchy?
items.answer: Cut the dendrogram at a chosen dissimilarity height (or use `fcluster` with `maxclust=k` in SciPy) so that exactly k vertical subtrees remain—each subtree is one cluster.

items.question: How does agglomerative clustering differ from divisive clustering?
items.answer: Agglomerative starts with n singleton clusters and merges upward until one remains; divisive starts with one cluster and splits downward until singletons. Agglomerative is cheaper to implement and far more common in libraries.

items.question: Ward linkage vs complete linkage on elongated blobs?
items.answer: Complete linkage resists merging until the farthest cross-pair is small, favoring compact clusters; Ward minimizes variance increase under Euclidean geometry. Single linkage merges on the nearest bridge and often chains elongated structures.
